speaker: interviewer
text: It's November 17, 1995. The survivor is Freda Weiss. This is tape five. Please continue, Mrs. Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- and In New York, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to do something. We didn't have any more money, because we lost it on the chicken farm. I went to work on Orchard Street. And I was happy that I can work. And they paid me pretty good. I was a good sales lady. I speak Spanish fluently, and there was Spanish customers a lot. My husband worked for a trucking company. And we made a living, with the two children. Ruthie went to college, art school. And she worked for Warner Brothers for two years in art-- art work. And Reba went to a Jewish day school in Brooklyn, New York. And being on Orchard Street, one of the owners, store owners, got killed. Somebody made a hold up on him and killed him. It was very rare to find, very hard to find an empty store to rent. I wanted to go on my own business, not to work for somebody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the landlord who owned that business. And I told him, look. Why shall I work for somebody else? I'm a good sales lady. I'm a buyer, I understand merchandise. Please rent me the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, huh. He showed me a list, 200 people who wants the store. I says, I understand. It's very hard to find here a store. He says, give me time. I have to pick one of those. And he put my name also there, Freda Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight days later, he comes to Goldman and Cohn, where I worked. He says, Freda is here? They say, yeah, Freda! Mr. Kipness calls you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I come out. He says, Freda, you are lucky. Your name is my mother's name. And this was the reason I picked you to rent to you the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amazing? My mother's name was Freda, and I loved her dearly. And she is no more here. So I want you to have the store. I took the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a lot of the money to buy merchandise. But I borrowed here and there. And I bought samples. Not an average like everybody else has, just samples-- gorgeous things, size 3, 5, 7, 9. I should be different than somebody else, so I'm going to have customers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have enough merchandise. And one Sunday, I sold out everything. So I had more money, I had profit. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lived there in an apartment. I paid high rent, New York is not-- is expensive And little by little, I made the other store. I put the men's and ladies, and here the children's wear. And I had a department store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freda's Clothing Store. I have the sign. I have the sign, even a picture. And it was about 15 years also there, in Orchard Street. In the meantime, at nighttime, I couldn't sleep, thinking all the time about concentration camp, about Auschwitz, about my mother, my darling mother, my sisters, the children, my aunt. I used to go from one end's town, literally every night, this house, this people, this one. Closing my eyes, and thinking, this people, to remember everybody, until I come to the synagogue. And I saw in the synagogue, my mother coming out with my father, together. My mother looked so beautiful. She was tall and gorgeous, very good woman. And I thought, I'm going to make a poem. I want this poem to be for my children. And I want to say it by organizations, in the Holocaust survivors meeting. I-- I did it many times. And they call me to meetings, where they find out that I have it. They call me, and I say it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say it for us, the poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would be very glad to say it to you. One second. [DRINKS] The poem is written by me. It took me a-- one year. Every night, I couldn't sleep. I used to light the lamp, the light, and write. And it says, it goes like that. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us, Mrs. Weiss, why you decided to make this tape and tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I am very talented in the field. I make songs. I compose songs. I know songs in the ghetto. I know songs from concentration camp. I know Israeli songs. You know. And I decided, because my children should have it. And-- and I love to attend organizations, and to be on the stage, and to share it. I-- I feel it's in me, and I can do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In closing, is there anything that you would like to say to all of those who will see this tape and hear your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a lot to say. I can say so much, that I am very happy now here in America. I'm happy that God helped me to have this [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] means the privilege to be in such a wonderful state, like country, like America. But one thing I beg the people, the generation, the coming generation, that they should say never again. They should know that there is still anti-Semitism in this country, also. And all over the country, they resist-- they just can't take the Jewish people. But I hope, since Israel is born, and Israel is a very beautiful Jewish country. Even it happened last time what the one Jew killed the prime minister. It was a crazy man. It wasn't normal. And they shouldn't take the symbol from that person. Because we going to be a country forever. Our generation going to be very happy to live there. And so is America, very nice country. I love it. And I thank for every inch, for every thing, what I have here. [NON-ENGLISH] gave me mazel to have beautiful two daughters, and five grandchildren. And my husband is sick. He is in a home. Which, I cannot help him. And they are taking very good care. He's in Borough Park, Brooklyn, in a home, Jewish. He's religious. And he needs the kosher home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thank for everybody. I'm not going to be long, and my generation is going to go. So it's going to come a new generation. And they should see to it that Jewish life shouldn't be a waste. Thank you, Mrs. Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Weiss, who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, on the left hand side, my father. His name was Mayor Marcus. And the right hand side is my mother, which went to Auschwitz. Her name is Rose Yankovitz. In the middle is my two sisters, Ethel and Sarah. And the three underneath is Menachem, Marcus in Israel, Rosie in Vienna, and myself, Freda Weiss, born Marcus, the little girl there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken? Where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I don't remember exactly. I was only four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in Khust, Czechoslovakia. This is my husband's family, his parents, mother, father, four brothers, which all are gone in concentration camps-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two sisters, and grandchildren for my husband's parents, the grandchildren. My husband's name is Henry Weiss, like I said. And he had also a family, but they are not here-- only his own pictures here. The rest, I don't have the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my mother holding the gravel and working in our beautiful garden. The grass was cut fresh. And my mother was graveling together the grass. And I am supposed to go to [NON-ENGLISH], which I like to go. I went in to help her, and to say goodbye to my dear mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was just a photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is this, Mrs. Weiss?

speaker: interviewee
text: That lovely picture is my dear mother. I loved her very much. And she perished in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know, really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wonderful man is the father for my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this photo was taken, and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's taken at Khust, Czechoslovakia. I don't know the date, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that beautiful woman was my husband's mother, who perished in the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Her name was [NON-ENGLISH], maiden name, Pearl. Do you know when this picture was taken, and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Weiss, who was in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is my husband, Henry, myself, Freda Weiss, my two daughters-- my lovely two daughters-- Ruth Weiss Levy, and Reba Weiss Soah. Reba lives in Seattle, Washington. And she is a lawyer. And Ruthie is a therapist-- massage therapist. Works for chiropracting doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is Reba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reba is near Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ruthie is close to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Ruthie's wedding day, 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me see-- when was the wedding? 1950, we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometime 1970s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Brooklyn, New York, where we lived, in Borough Park. And before she went to put on the dress to the wedding, we made a picture at home. And this clothes, what the girls are wearing, is from my store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This lovely young man is Ruthie's son, my grandson. His name is Matthew. And he calls me, my beautiful Bubbe. And this is taken in Brooklyn, New York, where they live, Marlborough Road. And he is a wonderful young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my handsome grandson, Gideon, Ruthie's son, second son. He just got bar mitzvah. And that tallit that he's wearing, my daughter Ruth made the tallits for him. And he calls me, my Bubbe who makes the best cookies in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who was in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This young man is the yummy, yummy Jonah, who is very sweet. He is only nine years old. And I love him very much. So I love all of them very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn, Marlborough Road, where they live. She expect that the third one should be a girl, because she has two bigger boys. But came another boy, and he is yummy!

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those lovely children are my second daughter's children, Reba Weiss Soah. Their name is Gabe and Molly. They live in Washington state, in Seattle. And I haven't seen them for a long time. They are much bigger now than the pictures are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Seattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you.
